en.content: A function $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined as $f(x)=x^2-4 x+5$ is
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } f: R \rightarrow R \\ & f(x)=x^2-4 x+5 \end{aligned}$$

One-One (Injective)

$$\begin{array}{l} & f\left(x_1\right) =f\left(x_2\right) \\ \Rightarrow & x_1^2-4 x_1+5 =x_1^2-4 x_2+5 \\ \Rightarrow & x_1^2-x_2^2 =4\left(x_1-x_2\right) \\ \Rightarrow & \left(x_1-x_2\right)\left(x_1+x_2\right) =4\left(x_1-x_2\right) \\ \Rightarrow & x_1+x_2 =4 \\ \Rightarrow & x_1 =4-x_2 \end{array}$$

Thus, $f(x)$ is not an injective mapping. onto surjective

$$\begin{array}{rlr} \text { let } & y =x^2-4 x+5 \\ \Rightarrow & y =(x-2)^2+1 \\ \Rightarrow & y-1 =(x-2)^2 \\ \Rightarrow & x =\sqrt{(y-1)}+2 \end{array}$$

Thus, for any value of $y<1, x \notin \mathrm{R}$. So, we don't have a pre-image for all $y \in \mathrm{R}$ in $x \in \mathrm{R}$.

Thus, $f(x)=x^2-4 x+5$ is not surjective.

en.content: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}a & c & -1 \\ b & 0 & 5 \\ 1 & -5 & 0\end{array}\right]$ is a skew-symmetric matrix, then the value of $2 a-(b+c)$ is
en.explanation:
We know that, if matrix A is skew symmetric, then

$$\begin{aligned} A^{\prime} & =-A \\ \therefore \quad\left[\begin{array}{ccc} a & b & 1 \\ c & 0 & -5 \\ -1 & 5 & 0 \end{array}\right] & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -a & -c & 1 \\ -b & 0 & -5 \\ -1 & 5 & 0 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$$

On comparing matrices, we get

$$\begin{aligned} a & =-a \Rightarrow 2 a=0 \Rightarrow a=0 \\ b & =-c \text { and } c=-b \end{aligned}$$

Now, $\quad 2 a+(b+c)=2 \times 0+(b-b)=0$

en.explanation:

$|\operatorname{adj} A|=|A|^{n-1}$

where $n$ is the order of square matrix $A$

$$\begin{array}{llrl} \text { Given } & & |\operatorname{adj} A| & =8 \\ \therefore & & |A|^{n-1} & =8 \\ \Rightarrow & & |A|^{3-1} & =8 \\ \Rightarrow & & |A|^2 & =8 \\ \Rightarrow & & |A| & =2 \sqrt{2} \end{array}$$

Also, determinant of a matrix and its transpose has same values.

en.explanation:
Given,

$$A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]$$

and $$A^{-1}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & \lambda & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]$$

Since, $A A^{-1}=I$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & \lambda & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ & {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 12-3 \lambda & 0 \\ 0 & -3+\lambda & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]} \end{aligned}$$

On comparing matrices, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 12-3 \lambda=0 \text { and }-3+\lambda=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=4 \text { and } \lambda=4 \end{aligned}$$

en.explanation:
Let $\quad u=e^{\sin ^2 x}$ and $v=\cos x$

$\therefore \quad \frac{d u}{d x}=e^{\sin ^2 x}(2 \sin x) \cos x$

and $$\frac{d v}{d x}=-\sin x$$

Thus,

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d u}{d v} & =\frac{d u / d x}{d v / d x} \\ & =\frac{e^{\sin ^2 x} \cdot 2 \sin x \cdot \cos x}{-\sin x} \\ & =-2 \cos x e^{\sin ^2 x} \end{aligned}$$

en.explanation:

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & f(x)=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{2}{x} \\ & f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{x^2} \\ & \text { Put, } \quad f^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{x^2}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}=\frac{2}{x^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x^2=4 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x= \pm 2 \\ & \text { Now, } \quad f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{6}{x^3} \\ & \text { At } x=2, \quad f^{\prime \prime}(2)=\frac{6}{8}>0 \\ & \text { At } x=-2, \quad f^{\prime \prime}(-2)=-\frac{6}{8}<0 \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Thus, } f(x) \text { has local minima at } x=2 \text {. } \end{aligned}$$

en.content:
(a) Verify whether the function $f$ defined by $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}x \sin \left(\frac{1}{x}\right), & x \neq 0 \\ 0, & x=0\end{array}\right.$ is continuous at $x=0$ or not.

OR

(b) Check for differentiability of the function $f$ defined by $f(x)=|x-5|$, at the point $x=5$.
en.explanation:
(a) Given, $$f(x)= \begin{cases}x \sin \left(\frac{1}{x}\right), & x \neq 0 \\ 0, & x=0\end{cases}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{for a continuous function,}\\ \mathrm{LHL} & =\mathrm{RHL}=f(a) \\ \text{Now,}\quad \mathrm{LHL} & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} x \sin \frac{1}{x} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}(0-h) \sin \left(\frac{1}{0-h}\right) \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}-h \sin \left(-\frac{1}{h}\right) \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} h \sin \left(\frac{1}{h}\right) \\ & =0 \times \sin (\infty) \\ & =0 \end{aligned}$$

and $$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{RHL} & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} x \sin \frac{1}{x} \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}(0+h) \sin \left(\frac{1}{0+h}\right) \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} h \sin \left(\frac{1}{h}\right) \\ & =0 \cdot \sin (\infty)=0 \end{aligned}$$

So, $\quad$ LHL $=$ RHL $=f(0)$

Hence, $f(x)$ is continuous at $x=0$

OR

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { (b) Given } \\ & f(x)=|x-5| \\ & \therefore \quad f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} (x-5) & x \geq 5 \\ -(x-5) & x<5 \end{array}\right. \end{aligned}$$

Here,

$$\begin{aligned} \text { LHD } & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(x-h)-f(x)}{-h} \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(5-h)-f(5)}{-h} \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-(5-h-5)-0}{-h} \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h}{-h}=-1 \end{aligned}$$

and

$$\begin{aligned} \text { RHD } & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(5+h)-f(5)}{h} \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{(5+h-5)-0}{h} \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h}{h} \\ & =1 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{llrl} \text { from (i), } & 2 =2 \pi r \frac{d r}{d t} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{d r}{d t} =\frac{1}{\pi r} \end{array}$$

Now, substituting the value of $\frac{d r}{d t}$ in eq. (ii) we get

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d c}{d t} & =2 \pi \frac{1}{\pi r}=\frac{2}{r} \\ \text{Now,}\quad\left.\frac{d C}{d t}\right|_{\text {at } r=5} & =\frac{2}{5} \\ & =0.4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s} \end{aligned}$$

Thus, circumference of circle increases at the rate of $0.4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$.

en.content: Find the vector equation of the line passing through the point $(2,3,-5)$ and making equal angles with the co-ordinate axes.
en.explanation:
Given $$\alpha=\beta=\gamma$$

Now,

$$\begin{aligned} & \cos ^2 \alpha+\cos ^2 \beta+\cos ^2 \beta=1 \\ & \therefore \quad 3 \cos ^2 \alpha=1\quad [\because \alpha=\beta=\gamma] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \text { or, }\quad\cos ^2 \alpha & =\frac{1}{3} \\ \text { or, }\quad\cos \alpha & = \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

Thus, $\cos \alpha=\cos \beta=\cos \gamma= \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=l=m=n$

Let required equation of line is $\vec{r}=\vec{a}+k \vec{b}$

Here,

$$\begin{aligned} \vec{a} & =2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k} \\ \vec{b} & = \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \end{aligned}$$

Thus, required equation is

$$\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}) \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})$$

en.content:
(a) Find $\frac{d y}{d x}$, , if $(\cos x)^y=(\cos y)^x$.

OR

(b) If $\sqrt{1-x^2}+\sqrt{1-y^2}=a(x-y)$, prove that $\frac{d y}{d x}=\sqrt{\frac{1-y^2}{1-x^2}}$.
en.explanation:
(a) Given, $\quad(\cos x)^y=(\cos y)^x$

Taking $\log$ on both sides, we get

$$y \log (\cos x)=x \log (\cos y)$$

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\frac{d y}{d x} \cdot \log (\cos x)+y \cdot\left(\frac{-\sin x}{\cos x}\right)=1 \cdot \log (\cos y)+x \cdot\left(\frac{-\sin y}{\cos y}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d y}{d x} \cdot \log (\cos x)-\frac{y \sin x}{\cos x} & =\log (\cos y)-\frac{x \sin y}{\cos y} \frac{d y}{d x} \\ \frac{d y}{d x}\left[\log (\cos x)+\frac{y \sin y}{\cos y}\right] & =\log (\cos y)+\frac{y \sin x}{\cos x} \\ \frac{d y}{d x}(\log \cos x+x \tan y) & =\log (\cos y)+y \tan x \\ \frac{d y}{d x} & =\frac{\log (\cos y)+y \tan x}{\log (\cos x)+\tan y} \end{aligned}$$

OR

(b) Given, $\sqrt{1-x^2}+\sqrt{1-y^2}=a(x-y)$

Put $$x=\sin \theta \text { and } y=\sin \phi$$

$$\therefore \quad \theta=\sin ^{-1} x \text { and } \phi=\sin ^{-1} y$$

Now, $\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 \theta}+\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 \phi}=a(\sin \theta-\sin \phi)$

$$\begin{aligned} \cos \theta+\cos \phi & =a(\sin \theta-\sin \phi) \\ 2 \cos \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right) & =2 a \cos \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right) \cdot \sin \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \frac{\cos \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right)} & =a \\ \cot \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right) & =a \\ \frac{\theta-\phi}{2} & =\cot ^{-1} a \\ \theta-\phi & =2 \cot ^{-1} a \\ \therefore \quad \sin ^{-1} x-\sin ^{-1} y & =2 \cot ^{-1} a \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Differentiating w.r.t. } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^2}} \frac{d y}{d x} & =0 \\ \frac{d y}{d x} & =\sqrt{\frac{1-y^2}{1-x^2}} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

Hence proved.

en.content: If $x=a \sin ^3 \theta, y=b \cos ^3 \theta$,then find $\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}$ at $\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}$.
en.explanation:
Given, $x=a \sin ^3 \theta$ and $y=a \cos ^3 \theta$

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d x}{d \theta}=3 a \sin ^2 \theta \cos \theta \\ & \frac{d y}{d \theta}=-3 a \cos ^2 \theta \sin \theta \\ & \text { and } \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-3 a \cos ^2 \theta \sin \theta}{3 a \sin ^2 \theta \cos \theta} \\ & =-\cot \theta \\ & \text { and } \quad \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=\frac{d}{d \theta}(-\cot \theta) \frac{d \theta}{d x} \\ & =\operatorname{cosec}^2 \theta \frac{1}{3 a \sin ^2 \theta \cos \theta} \\ & =\frac{1}{3 a \sin ^4 \theta \cos \theta} \\ & \text { Now, }\left.\quad \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right|_{\text {at } x=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{1}{3 \cdot a\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^4 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}} \\ & =\frac{1}{3 \cdot a \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}=\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{3 a} \end{aligned}$$

en.content:
(a) Evaluate: $\int_0^\pi \frac{e^{\cos x}}{e^{\cos x}+e^{-\cos x}} d x$

OR

(b) Find: $\int \frac{2 x+1}{(x+1)^2(x-1)} d x$
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Let}\quad & I=\int_0^\pi \frac{e^{\cos x}}{e^{\cos x}+e^{-\cos x}} d x \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ \therefore\quad & I=\int_0^\pi \frac{e^{\cos (\pi-x)}}{e^{\cos (\pi-x)}+e^{-\cos (\pi-x)} d x} \\ & {\left[\text { Using } \int_0^\pi f(x) d x=\int_0^\pi f(a-x) d x\right]} \\ \text{or,}\quad & I=\int_0^\pi \frac{e^{-\cos x}}{e^{-\cos x}+e^{\cos x}} d x\quad\text{... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

On adding eqs. (i) \& (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} 2 I & =\int_0^\pi \frac{e^{\cos x}+e^{-\cos x}}{e^{\cos x}+e^{-\cos x}} d x \\ & =\int_0^\pi d x \\ & =[x]_0^\pi=\pi \\ \therefore \quad I & =\frac{\pi}{2} \end{aligned}$$

OR

(b) Let $$I=\int \frac{2 x+1}{(x+1)^2(x-1)} d x$$

Let $\frac{2 x+1}{(x+1)^2(x-1)}=\frac{A}{(x+1)}+\frac{B}{(x+1)^2}+\frac{C}{(x-1)}$

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 x+1=A(x+1)(x-1)+B(x-1)+C(x+1)^2 \\ & 2 x+1=A\left(x^2-1\right)+B x-B+C\left(x^2+2 x+1\right) \end{aligned}$$

On comparing, we get

$$A+C=0 ; B+2 C=2 \text { and }-A-B+C=1$$

On solving, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & A=\frac{-3}{4}, B=\frac{1}{2} \text { and } C=\frac{3}{4} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{2 x+1}{(x+1)^2(x-1)}=\frac{-3}{4(x+1)}+\frac{1}{2(x+1)^2}+\frac{3}{4(x-1)} \end{aligned}$$

Thus, $\int \frac{2 x+1}{(x+1)^2(x-1)} d x$

$$\begin{aligned} & =-\frac{3}{4} \int \frac{1}{(x+1)} d x+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{(x+1)^2} d x+\frac{3}{4} \int \frac{1}{(x-1)} d x \\ & =-\frac{3}{4} \log |x+1|+\frac{1}{2} I_1+\frac{3}{4} \log |x-1|+C_1 \end{aligned}$$

How, $\quad I_1=\int \frac{1}{(x+1)^2} d x$

let $x+1=u \Rightarrow d x=d u$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad I_1 & =\int \frac{1}{u^2} d u=\frac{u^{-2+1}}{-2+1}+C_2 \\ & =-\frac{1}{u}+C_2=-\frac{1}{(x+1)}+C_2 \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, $\int \frac{2 x+1}{(x+1)^2(x-1)} d x$

$$=-\frac{3}{4} \log |x+1|-\frac{1}{2(x+1)}+\frac{3}{4} \log |x-1|+\mathrm{C}$$

Where $C=C_1+C_2$

en.content:
(a) Find the particular solution of the differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}-2 x y=3^{x^2} e^{x^2} ; y(0)=5$.

OR

(b) Solve the following differential equation: $$ x^2 d y+y(x+y) d x=0 $$
en.explanation:
(a) Given differential equation is

$$\frac{d y}{d x}-2 x y=3 x^2 e^{x^2}$$

On comparing the above equation with

$$\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q$$

We get $P=-2 x, Q=3 x^2 e^{x^2}$

$$\therefore \quad I=e^{\int P d x}=e^{-\int 2 x d x}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =e^{-2\left(\frac{x^2}{2}\right)}=e^{-x^2} \\ y \cdot e^{-x^2} & =\int 3 x^2 e^{x^2} \cdot\left(e^{-x^2}\right) d x+C \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{or,}\quad y \cdot e^{-x^2} & =3 \int x^2 d x+C \\ \text{or, }\quad \frac{y}{e^{x^2}} & =3\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]+C \end{aligned}$$

or, $$\frac{y}{e^{x^2}}=x^3+C$$

or, $$y=e^{x^2} x^3+C e^{x^2}$$

Given $$y(0)=5$$

$$\therefore \quad 5=0+C e^0 \Rightarrow C=5$$

Thus, required solution is

or $$y=e^{x^2}\left(x^3+5\right)$$

OR

(b) We have,

$$\begin{aligned} x^2 d y+y(x+y) d x & =0 \\ \frac{x^2}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}+(x+y) & =0 \\ \frac{x^2}{y^2} \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{x}{y}+1 & =0\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Put }\quad y & =v x \\ \Rightarrow\quad\frac{d y}{d x} & =V+x \frac{d V}{d x} \end{aligned}$$

From eq (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & & \frac{1}{V^2}\left(V+x \frac{d V}{d x}\right)+\frac{1}{V}+1 & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & & x \frac{d V}{d x} & =-\left(2 V+V^2\right) \\ \Rightarrow & & \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{V}-\frac{1}{V+2}\right] d V & =-\frac{d x}{x} \end{aligned}$$

On integrating both sides, we get

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}(\log V-\log V+2)=-\log x+C_1$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & \frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{V}{V+2}\right)=-\log x+C_1 \\ \Rightarrow & \log \left(\frac{V}{V+2}\right)=-2 \log x+\log k \end{array}$$

where $k=e^{2 C_1}$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & & \log \left(\frac{V}{V+2}\right) & =\log \left(\frac{k}{x^2}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & & \frac{V}{V+2} & =\frac{k}{x^2} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting $V=\frac{y}{x}$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{y}{2 x+y}=\frac{k}{x^2} \\ & \Rightarrow x^2 y=C^2(2 x+y) \text { where } k=C^2 \end{aligned}$$

en.content: Find a vector of magnitude 4 units perpendicular to each of the vectors $2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}$ and hence verify your answer.
en.explanation:
Given vectors are :

$$\begin{aligned} \vec{a} & =2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k} \\ \vec{b} & =\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k} \\ \vec{a} \times \vec{b} & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & -1 \end{array}\right| \\ & =\hat{i}(1-1)-\hat{j}(-2-1)+\hat{k}(2+1) \\ & =3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Now, }\\ &\begin{aligned} |\vec{a} \times \vec{b}| & =\sqrt{(3)^2+(3)^2}=\sqrt{9+9} \\ & =\sqrt{18}=3 \sqrt{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Therefore required vector is, } 4\left(\frac{\vec{a} \times \vec{b}}{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|}\right)\\ &\begin{aligned} & =\frac{4}{3 \sqrt{2}}(3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) \\ & =\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3} \times 3(\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \\ & =2 \sqrt{2}(\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

en.content:
(a) If $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 0 & -3 \\ 1 & 2 & 0\end{array}\right]$, then find $A^{-1}$ and hence solve the following system of equations:

$$\begin{array}{r} x+2 y-3 z=1 \\ 2 x-3 z=2 \\ x+2 y=3 \end{array}$$

OR

(b) Find the product of the matrices $\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 3 & 2 \\ 3 & -3 & -4\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc}-6 & 17 & 13 \\ 14 & 5 & -8 \\ -15 & 9 & -1\end{array}\right]$ and hence solve the system of linear equations:

$$\begin{aligned} x+2 y-3 z & =4 \\ 2 x+3 y+2 z & =2 \\ 3 x-3 y-4 z & =11 \end{aligned}$$
en.explanation:
Given,

$$\begin{aligned} A & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 0 & -3 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ |A| & =1(0+6)-2(0+3)-3(4-0) \\ & =6-6-12=-12 \neq 0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow \text { inverse exists co-factors are }\\ &\begin{aligned} & C_{11}=(-1)^2\left|\begin{array}{cc} 0 & -3 \\ 2 & 0 \end{array}\right|=6 \\ & C_{12}=(-1)^3\left|\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -3 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right|=-3 \\ & C_{13}=(-1)^4\left|\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 \end{array}\right|=4 \\ & C_{21}=(-1)^3\left|\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 0 \end{array}\right|=-6 \\ & C_{22}=(-1)^4\left|\begin{array}{ll} 1 & -3 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right|=3 \\ & C_{23}=(-1)^5\left|\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & C_{31}=(-1)^4\left|\begin{array}{ll} 2 & -3 \\ 0 & -3 \end{array}\right|=-6 \\ & C_{32}=(-1)^5\left|\begin{array}{ll} 1 & -3 \\ 2 & -3 \end{array}\right|=-3 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &C_{33}=(-1)^6\left|\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 0 \end{array}\right|=-4\\ &\text { The adjoint of the matrix } \mathrm{A} \text { is given by }\\ &\begin{aligned} \operatorname{adj}(A) & =\left[\begin{array}{lll} C_{11} & C_{21} & C_{31} \\ C_{12} & C_{22} & C_{32} \\ C_{13} & C_{23} & C_{33} \end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & -6 & -6 \\ -3 & 3 & -3 \\ 4 & 0 & -4 \end{array}\right] \\ \therefore \quad A^{-1} & =\frac{(\operatorname{adj} A)}{|A|}=-\frac{1}{12}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & -6 & -6 \\ -3 & 3 & -3 \\ 4 & 0 & -4 \end{array}\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{12}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -6 & 6 & 6 \\ 3 & -3 & 3 \\ -4 & 0 & 4 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

Given system of linear equations are

$$\begin{array}{r} x+2 y-3 z=1 \\ 2 x-3 z=2 \\ x+2 y=3 \end{array}$$

Represent it in matrix form

$$\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 0 & -3 \\ 0 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]$$

Which is in the form $A X=B$

$$X=A^{-1} B$$

$$\begin{aligned} &=\frac{1}{12}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -6 & 6 & 6 \\ 3 & -3 & 3 \\ -4 & 0 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right] \\ &=\frac{1}{12}\left[\begin{array}{c} 24 \\ 6 \\ 8 \end{array}\right] \\ &=\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ \frac{1}{2} \\ \frac{2}{3} \end{array}\right] \\ & \therefore \quad\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ \frac{1}{2} \\ \frac{2}{3} \end{array}\right] \\ & \text { or, } x=2, y=\frac{1}{2} \text { and } z=\frac{2}{3} \\ & \text { OR } \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { (b) We have, }\\ &\begin{aligned} A B & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 3 & 2 \\ 3 & -3 & -4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -6 & 17 & 13 \\ 14 & 5 & -8 \\ -15 & 9 & -1 \end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -6+28+45 & 17+10-27 & 13-16+3 \\ -12+42-30 & 34+15+18 & 26-24-2 \\ -18-42+60 & 51-15-36 & 39+24+4 \end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 67 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 67 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 67 \end{array}\right] \\ & =67\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} \text { Thus, } & A B =67 I \\ \Rightarrow & A\left(\frac{1}{67} B\right) =I \\ \Rightarrow & A^{-1} =\frac{1}{67}(B) \end{array}$$

Given system of linear equation is

$$\begin{aligned} x+2 y-3 z & =-4 \\ 2 x+3 y+2 z & =2 \\ 3 x-3 y-4 z & =11 \end{aligned}$$

Represent it in matrix form as

$$\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 3 & 2 \\ 3 & -3 & -4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} -4 \\ 2 \\ 11 \end{array}\right]$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { which is of the form } A X=D\\ &\therefore X=A^{-1} D\\ &\begin{aligned} &=\frac{1}{67}(B) \cdot D \\ &=\frac{1}{67}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -6 & 17 & 13 \\ 14 & 5 & -8 \\ -15 & 9 & -1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} -4 \\ 2 \\ 11 \end{array}\right] \\ &=\frac{1}{67}\left[\begin{array}{c} 201 \\ -134 \\ 67 \end{array}\right] \\ &=\left[\begin{array}{c} 3 \\ -2 \\ 1 \end{array}\right] \\ & \therefore x=3, y=-2 \text { and } z=1 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

en.content:
(a) Find the co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point $(2,3,-8)$ to the line $\frac{4-x}{2}=\frac{y}{6}=\frac{1-z}{3}$.

OR

(b). Find the shortest distance between the lines $\mathrm{L}_1 \& \mathrm{~L}_2$ given below: $\mathrm{L}_1$ : The line passing through $(2,-1,1)$ and parallel to $\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{1}=\frac{z}{3} \mathrm{~L}_2: \vec{r}=\hat{i}+(2 \mu+1) \hat{j}-(\mu+2) \hat{k}$.
en.explanation:
(a) Given line is $\frac{4-x}{2}=\frac{y}{6}=\frac{1-z}{3}$

or, $\quad \frac{x-4}{-2}=\frac{y}{6}=\frac{z-1}{-3}=\lambda$

Now, the co-ordinates of any points $Q$ on the line are $(-2 \lambda+4,6 \lambda,-3 \lambda+1)$.

Also, the given point is $p(2,3,-8)$

The direction ratios of PQ are $-2 \lambda+4-2,6 \lambda-3,-3 \lambda$

$$+1+8 \text { i.e., }-2 \lambda+2,6 \lambda-3,-3 \lambda+9$$

Also, the direction cosines of the given line are $-2,6-3$.

If $\mathrm{PQ} \perp$ line, then

$$\begin{aligned} -2(-2 \lambda+2)+6(6 \lambda-3)-3(-3 \lambda+9) & =0 \\ 4 \lambda-4+36 \lambda-18+9 \lambda-27 & =0 \\ 49 \lambda-49 & =0 \\ \lambda & =1 \end{aligned}$$

Now, the foot of the perpendicular is

$$[-2(1)+4,6(1),-3(1)+1] \text { i.e., }(2,6,-2)$$

Hence, the distance PQ is

$$\begin{aligned} & =\sqrt{(2-2)^2+(3-6)^2+(-8+2)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{0+9+36}=\sqrt{45}=3 \sqrt{5} \text { units } \end{aligned}$$

OR

(b) Given, $\mathrm{L}_1$ : The line passing through $(2,-1,1)$ and parallel to $\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{1}=\frac{z}{3}\quad\text{.... (i)}$

Direction ratios of line (i) are $(1,1,3)$

$\therefore$ Vector equation of line passing through $(2,-1,1)$ having direction ratios is

$\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})\quad\text{.... (i)}$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Given, } & & \mathrm{L}_2: \vec{r} & =\hat{i}+(3 \mu+1) \hat{j}-(\mu+2) \hat{k} \\ \text { or, } & & \vec{r} & =(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k})+\mu(3 \hat{j}-\hat{k})\quad\text{.... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

Now, shortest distance between lines (ii) \& (iii) is given by

Here, $\quad \overrightarrow{a_1}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \overrightarrow{a_2}=(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k})$

$$d=\frac{\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right) \cdot\left(\vec{a}_2 \times \vec{a}_1\right)}{\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right|}$$

$$\overrightarrow{b_1}=(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}), \overrightarrow{b_2}=(3 \hat{j}-\hat{k})$$

Now,

$$\begin{array}{r} \overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}=(1-2) \hat{i}+(1+1) \hat{j}+(-2-1) \hat{k} \\ =-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k} \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{b_1} \times \overrightarrow{b_2} & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 3 & -1 \end{array}\right| \\ & =\hat{i}(-1-9)-\hat{j}(-1-0)+\hat{k}(3-0) \\ & =-10 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { and } \quad \left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right| & =\sqrt{(-10)^2+(1)^2+(3)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{100+1+9} \\ & =\sqrt{110} \\ \therefore \quad d & =\left|\frac{(-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})(-10 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{110}}\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{10+2-3}{\sqrt{110}}\right|=\frac{9}{\sqrt{110}} \text { unit } \end{aligned}$$

en.content:
A departmental store sends bills to charge its customers once a month. Past experience shows that $70 \%$ of its customers pay their first month bill in time. The store also found that the customer who pays the bill in time has the probability of 0.8 of paying in time next month and the customer who doesn't pay in time has the probability of 0.4 of paying in time the next month.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

(i) Let $E_1$ and $E_2$ respectively, denote the event of customer paying or not paying the first month bill in time.

(ii) Let A denotes the event of customer paying second month's bill in time, then find $P\left(A \mid E_1\right)$ and $P\left(A \mid E_2\right)$.

(iii) Find the probability of customer paying second month's bill in time.

OR

(iii) Find the probability of customer paying first month's bill in time if it is found that customer has paid the second month's bill in time.
en.explanation:
(i) $P\left(E_1\right)=$ Probability that customer pyas the bill in first time

$P\left(E_2\right)=$ Probability that customer does not pay the bill in first time.

$\therefore \quad P\left(E_1\right)=\frac{70}{100}=\frac{7}{10}$

and $$P\left(E_2\right)=\frac{30}{100}=\frac{3}{10}$$

(ii) $A=$ Event that customer pay the bill in $2^{\text {nd }}$ month on time

$$\begin{aligned} P\left(\frac{A}{E_1}\right) & =0.8=\frac{8}{10} \\ \text { and } \quad P\left(\frac{A}{E_2}\right) & =0.4=\frac{4}{10} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{(iii)}\quad P(A) & =P\left(E_1\right) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{E_1}\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{E_2}\right) \\ & =\frac{7}{10} \times \frac{8}{10}+\frac{3}{10} \times \frac{4}{10} \\ & =\frac{56}{100}+\frac{12}{100} \\ & =\frac{68}{100}=\frac{17}{25} \end{aligned}$$

OR

$P\left(\frac{E_1}{A}\right)=\frac{P\left(E_1\right) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{E_1}\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{E_1}\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{E_2}\right)}$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{\frac{7}{10} \times \frac{8}{10}}{\frac{7}{10} \times \frac{8}{10}+\frac{3}{10} \times \frac{4}{10}} \\ & =\frac{\frac{56}{100}}{\frac{68}{100}}=\frac{56}{68}=\frac{14}{17} \end{aligned}$$

The random variable $X$ has the following probability distribution where $a$ and $b$ are some constants:

X 1 2 3 4 5
P(X) 0.2 a a 0.2 b

If the mean $E(X)=3$, then find values of $a$ and $b$ and hence determine $P(X \geq 3)$.
en.explanation:
We have,

X 1 2 3 4 5
P(X) 0.2 a a 0.2 b

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Given }\quad &E(X)=3\\ &\text { Since, } \quad E(X)=\sum X P(X) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore & & 3 & =0.2+2 a+3 a+0.8+5 b \\ \text { or, } & & 3 & =5 a+5 b+1 \\ & \text { or, } & 5 a+5 b & =2 \quad\text{... (i)}\\ & \text { Also, } & \Sigma P(X) & =1 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{r} \therefore & 0.2+a+a+0.2+b =1 \\ \text { or, } & 2 a+b =1-0.4 \\ \text { or, } & 2 a+b =0.6\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{array}$$

eq (i) $\times 5-$ eq (i), we get

$$a=\frac{1}{5}=0.2$$

Substituting value of a in eq (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} 5\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)+5 b & =2 \\ 5 b & =1 \\ b & =\frac{1}{5}=0.2 \end{aligned}$$

Thus, $a=0.2$ and $b=0.2$

Now,

$$\begin{aligned} P(X \geq 3) & =P(X=3)+P(X=4)+P(X=5) \\ & =a+0.2+b \\ & =0.2+0.2+0.2 \\ & =0.6 \end{aligned}$$

37. A rectangular visiting card is to contain $24 \mathrm{sq} . \mathrm{cm}$. of printed matter. The margins at the top and bottom of the card are to be 1 cm and the margins on the left and right are to be $1 \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~cm}$ as shown below:

On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions:

(i) Write the expression for the area of the visiting card in terms of $x$.

(ii) Obtain the dimensions of the card of minimum area.
en.explanation:
(i) Given, $x=$ length of printed area

$y=$ breadth of printed area

Area of printed part $=x y$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \text { or, }\quad 24 & =x y \\ \text { or, }\quad y & =\frac{24}{x}\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

Now, area of visiting card $=(x+3)(y+2)$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{or, }\quad & A=(x+3)(y+2) \\ \text{or, }\quad & A=(x+3)\left(\frac{24}{x}+2\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \text { or, }\quad & A=\frac{(x+3)(24+2 x)}{x} \\ \text { or, }\quad & A=\frac{2 x^2+30 x+72}{x} \\ \text { or, }\quad & A=2 x+30+\frac{72}{x} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { (ii) We have, Area, } A=2 x+30+\frac{72}{x}\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{d A}{d x}=2+0-\frac{72}{x^2} \\ \text { and } & \frac{d^2 A}{d x^2}=\frac{144}{x^3} \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

for minimum area, put $\frac{d A}{d x}=0$

i.e., $$2-\frac{72}{x^2}=0$$

or, $$x^2=36 \Rightarrow x= \pm 6$$

At $x=6 \cdot \frac{d^2 A}{d x^2}>0$ Hence, minimum

Therefore, length of vising card

$$\begin{aligned} & =x+3=6+3=9 \mathrm{~cm} \\ \text { and breadth of visiting card }\quad & =y+2=\frac{24}{x}+2 \\ & =\frac{24}{6}+2=6 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$$

Max $$Z=60 x+40 y$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Subject to } \quad x+2 y & \leq 12 \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ 2 x+y & \leq 12 \quad\text{.... (ii)}\\ 4 x+5 y & \geq 20 \quad\text{.... (iii)}\\ x, y & \geq 0 \end{aligned}$$

Corner points $Z=60x+40y$
A(0, 4) 1600
B(0, 6) 2400
C(4, 4) 4000 (max)
D(6, 0) 3600
E(5, 0) 3000

Thus, maximum value of Z is 4000 which occurs at the point C(4, 4).

en.content: Find the area of the region bounded by the curve $4 x^2$ $+y^2=36$ using integration.
en.explanation:
Given curve is $4 x^2+y^2=36$

or, $\quad \frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{36}=1$

or, $\quad \frac{x^2}{3^2}+\frac{y^2}{6^2}=1$

since, ellipse is symmetrical along

$x$-axis and $y$-axis

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Area of ellipse } & =\text { Area of ABCD } \\ & =4 \times \text { Area of OBC } \\ & =4 \times \int_0^3 y d x \\ & =4 \times \int_0^3\left(2 \sqrt{9-x^2}\right) d x \\ & =8 \int_0^3 \sqrt{9-x^2} d x \end{aligned}$$

[since OBC is above $x$-axis]

$$=8 \int_0^3 \sqrt{3^2-x^2} d x$$

$$=8\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{3^2-x^2}+\frac{3^2}{2} \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{3}\right]_0^3$$

$$=8\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{9-x^2}+\frac{9}{2} \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{3}\right]_0^3$$

$$=8\left[0+\frac{9}{2} \sin ^{-1}(1)-(0+0)\right]$$

$$=8 \times \frac{9}{2} \times \frac{\pi}{2}$$

$$=2 \times 9 \pi$$

$$=18 \pi \text { sq. units }$$

(a) Students of a school are taken to a railway museum to learn about railways heritage and its history.

An exhibit in the museum depicted many rail lines on the track near the railway station. Let $L$ be the set of all rail lines on the railway track and $R$ be the relation on $L$ defined by

$R=\left\{l_1, l_2\right): l_1$ is parallel to $\left.l_2\right\}$

On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions:

(i) Find whether the relation R is symmetric or not.

(ii) Find whether the relation R is transitive or not.

(iii) If one of the rail lines on the railway track is represented by the equation $y=3 x+2$, then find the set of rail lines in R related to it.

OR

(b) Let $S$ be the relation defined by $S=\left\{\left(l_1, l_2\right): l_1\right.$ is perpendicular to $l_2$ \} check whether the relation $S$ is symmetric and transitive.
en.explanation:
(a) (i) Given Relation, $R=\left\{\left(l_1, l_2\right): d_1\right.$ is parallel to $l_2$

$$\because l_1\left\|l_2 \Rightarrow l_2\right\| l_1$$

Hence, given relation is symmetric.

(ii) Let $l_1 \| l_2$ and $l_2\left\|l_3 \Rightarrow l_1\right\| l_3$

[As parallel lines are parallel to each other]

(iii) Given equation of line : $y=3 x+2$

Comparing with $y=m x+C$

$$m=\text { slope }=3$$

Slope of parallel, lines is same

$\therefore$ Equation of set of rall line is given by

$$y=3 x+\lambda \quad \lambda \in R$$

OR

(b) Given relation, $S=\left\{\left(l_1, l_2\right): l_1\right.$ is perpendicular to $\left.l_2\right\}$

Symmetric $l_1 \perp l_2 \Rightarrow l_2 \perp l_1$

(Two lines are perpendicular to each other)

Hence, S is symmetric.

Transitive:

Let $L_1 \perp L_2$ and $l_2 \perp l_3 \Rightarrow l_1 \| l_3$

Thus, relation $S$ is not transitive.
